parentQuestion: A patient with cystic fibrosis has thick, dehydrated mucus clogging the airways ...

options: Differences in solute concentration creating an osmotic gradient
scaffolds.explain: Osmosis is driven by differences in SOLUTE concentration across a semipermeable membrane. Water moves from LOW solute (high water) to HIGH solute (low water) concentration to equalize osmotic pressure. this is PASSIVE; no ATP needed. See this through:: when Cl⁻ is secreted into mucus, it creates an osmotic gradient that PULLS water out of cells. No Cl⁻ secretion? No osmotic pull. No water movement. Receive this insight:this: osmosis is a passive consequence of solute gradients.

scaffolds.q: What does CFTR stand for in cystic fibrosis?
scaffolds.explain: CFTR stands for Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator — it's a CHLORIDE CHANNEL (and regulator of other ion channels). Embark on this:: it sits in the cell membrane and allows Cl⁻ to flow OUT of cells into secretions. When it's mutated (most common: ΔF508 deletion), Cl⁻ can't be secreted → secretions become dehydrated. Make room for this understanding:this: one protein, one job (Cl⁻ transport), catastrophic consequences when broken.
